Collection › Song Dynasty › ro-song-006 — Ji Zhou Meiping Vase with Sgraffito Plum Blossom

Reference Object ro-song-006

Ji Zhou Meiping Vase with Sgraffito Plum Blossom Decoration

吉州窑 梅瓶 — Southern Song Dynasty, 1127–1279 CE

Southern Song Ji Zhou Ware Meiping Sgraffito Black Iron Glaze Three-Layer Structure Dragon Kiln GOM Examined

Object Description

A Meiping vase of classic form with a small mouth, short neck, broad rounded shoulder, tapering body, and a broad flat foot ring. The exterior is covered with an iron-rich calcium-alkaline black glaze applied over a sericite-rich stoneware body. The decorative programme consists of a plum blossom branch in sgraffito technique: the glaze is carved to reveal the pale body beneath, with iron-brown pigment detailing the stamens and flower centres.

The interior of the mouth and neck are glazed. The foot ring is broad and unglazed, exposing the characteristic ochre-buff stoneware body of Ji Zhou production. The base interior retains traces of the original firing environment including kiln sand adhesion and partial glaze coverage consistent with dragon kiln firing practice.

Microscopic Diagnostic Analysis

The vase was examined using Gemmological Optical Microscopy (GOM) under reflected and oblique illumination at magnifications from 10× to 100×. The investigation focused on the sgraffito layer architecture, crackle morphology, glaze–body interface, foot-rim characteristics, and firing traces. The object preserves a complete technological system produced during a single high-temperature firing and shows no evidence of restoration or later intervention.

The Ji Zhou sgraffito technique requires that all three decorative layers — ground glaze, carved body exposure, and iron pigment detailing — be present and formed before firing. Their simultaneous maturation within a single firing cycle is the primary authentication criterion for this decorative type.

1. Three-Layer Sgraffito Architecture

The most important authentication criterion is the preserved stratigraphy of the decoration. GOM confirms three original technological layers formed before firing and matured simultaneously within a single firing cycle:

Sgraffito Layer Architecture — Pre-Firing Sequence

1

Iron-Rich Black Glaze Ground

Calcium-alkaline iron-rich glaze applied over the entire body. Deep glossy black with warm brown reflections under oblique illumination. Homogeneous vitreous texture without secondary coatings.

2

Exposed Stoneware Body (Carved Field)

Light beige to ochre stoneware body exposed by carving through the glaze before firing. Medium-grained texture with visible quartz inclusions and sericite-rich clay fabric. Corresponds to excavated Yonghe kiln material.

3

Iron-Brown Pigment (Floral Details)

Iron-rich pigment applied to flower centres and stamen areas before firing. Particles embedded within the glaze matrix with complete firing integration. No evidence of later overpainting or surface application.

2. Glaze–Sgraffito Interface

The transition between the black glaze ground and the carved decoration is diagnostically significant. Microscopy reveals rounded glaze margins, slight glaze flow along carved edges, smooth firing-softened boundaries, and complete absence of sharp mechanical cuts. This morphology confirms that carving was performed before glazing and firing. Post-firing carving would produce sharp angular cuts without glaze flow or rounded margins.

3. Natural Crackle Network

A fine homogeneous crackle network extends across both exterior and interior glaze surfaces. Uniform crack density, continuous propagation, mineral-filled crack channels with gradual darkening of interiors, and absence of mechanically induced fractures characterise the crackle. The morphology is consistent with natural thermal contraction during cooling followed by centuries of mineral infiltration through capillary action.

4. Iron-Rich Black Glaze

The glaze exhibits deep glossy black colour with warm brown reflections under oblique illumination, strong optical depth, homogeneous vitreous texture, and absence of secondary coatings. These optical properties are consistent with calcium-alkaline glazes containing high concentrations of iron oxide fired under controlled reducing conditions in a dragon kiln environment.

5. Stoneware Body

The exposed ceramic body in the carved fields displays light beige to ochre colour, medium-grained texture, visible quartz inclusions, and sericite-rich clay fabric without artificial colouring. These characteristics correspond closely to excavated material from Yonghe kiln sites in Ji Zhou (Jiangxi province) and distinguish authentic Ji Zhou production from later imitations using different clay bodies.

6. Foot-Ring and Kiln Sand Adhesion

The broad unglazed foot ring displays natural firing colour, irregular handmade finishing, glaze termination with slight pooling, and complete absence of grinding or reshaping. Small refractory particles from the kiln floor remain partially fused into the ceramic surface of the base in random distribution, representing direct evidence of dragon kiln firing technology practised at Yonghe.

7. Restoration Assessment

Complete microscopic examination detected no restoration. No evidence was found for repainting, glaze replacement, fills, adhesive repairs, or reconstructed decoration. The object preserves its original technological integrity throughout.

Diagnostic Interpretation

Authentication is supported by the agreement of multiple independent technological observations reconstructing the complete manufacturing sequence from carving and glazing through high-temperature dragon kiln firing to long-term natural ageing. The internal consistency of all observations provides strong evidence for authentic Ji Zhou kiln production.

Key Diagnostic Markers

Three-layer sgraffito structure (glaze / body / iron pigment)
Rounded glaze-flow margins — pre-firing carving confirmed
Iron-brown pigment embedded within glaze matrix (firing-integrated)
Natural age-related crackle with mineral-filled channels
Iron-rich calcium-alkaline black glaze
Sericite-rich stoneware body with quartz inclusions
Broad unglazed foot ring — natural firing colour, no reshaping
Dragon kiln sand adhesion on base
No restoration detected — original technological integrity
High internal diagnostic consistency

Diagnostic Confidence: Very High

Comparative References

Published studies of authenticated Ji Zhou kiln material from Yonghe excavations, including analysis of sgraffito technique stratigraphy and iron-rich calcium-alkaline glaze chemistry, were consulted for comparative calibration of GOM observations. Full citations to be added.
